starting_state.K: Reader expects generic B2B software claims.
starting_state.P: Reader evaluates tools through vague efficiency language.

target_state.K: Reader sees the difference between dashboard language and real close-process knowledge.
target_state.P: Reader can map the product to close tasks like approvals and reconciliations.

displacement.K: Shift from category marketing to operational understanding.
displacement.P: Shift from broad evaluation to workflow-level evaluation.
displacement.A: Shift from suspicion to practical attention.

layers.assert: Acknowledge that finance teams are tired of visibility claims.
layers.exception: Break the dashboard frame by naming what teams actually need.
layers.log: Leave the reader with a believable operator-facing takeaway.

modules.name: old-belief-index
logic: Name what finance teams do not need.
modules.interface_hook: The real job is buried in the close process.

think_aloud_frames: "This will still show up at month-end, not during real close pain."

modules.name: expectation-break
logic: Contrast visibility claims with operational pain.
modules.interface_hook: So the copy has to prove operational literacy.

cta.critical_incident: Month-end still depends on late spreadsheet checks and follow-up messages even when the product promises visibility.
cta.absurd_old_conclusion: If visibility language were enough, controllers would not still be chasing approvals across tabs.
cta.switch_parameter: The decisive parameter is close workflow literacy, not abstract product polish.
cta.collapse_signal: The dashboard story stops being persuasive once the real close steps are named.

modules.name: new-framework
logic: Reframe the product around close tasks.
modules.interface_hook: That lens only works if the details feel lived-in.

cta.goal: Judge the product like a finance operator, not like a software buyer reading adjectives.
operations: Check whether the copy names close steps.
Compare claims to approval and reconciliation work.
methods: Use operational nouns before product adjectives.
Anchor the value in ERP, spreadsheets, and approvals.
Prefer workflow proof to category polish.
selection_rules: If the copy says visibility but names no close task, treat it as generic.
If the copy names approvals, reconciliations, and month-end friction, keep reading.

modules.name: boundary-and-timing-anchor
logic: Name spreadsheets, ERP checks, approvals, and month-end.
modules.interface_hook: Now the claim can land without hype.

timeline.now: Look for whether the copy names the actual close workflow.
timeline.soon: Check whether the next draft adds one more operator-level step.
timeline.later: Expect proof that the product reduces month-end chasing, not just dashboard language.

cta.checklist_template: This week I will scan finance copy for ____ workflow step. If it is missing, I will mark the draft as ____.
cta.downgrade_condition: If the copy stays at visibility and efficiency language after revision, downgrade the claim to generic B2B marketing.

modules.name: identity-embed
modules.output_state: Reader can repeat the value in their own workflow terms

rebuttal_lines: "Finance teams do not need another visibility promise; they need fewer month-end checks."
"If the copy cannot name approvals and reconciliations, it does not understand the close."
"Operator credibility starts where dashboard language ends."
retell_bullets: This is close-process copy, not generic SaaS copy.
The proof is in the workflow nouns.
Month-end detail is the credibility test.
cta.identity_lock_sentence: Although the category language sounds polished, I choose to judge finance copy by whether it understands the close.

spec.objective: Make the copy sound like it understands month-end close work.

diagnose.main_issue: The draft sounds like category marketing instead of close-operations knowledge.

rewrite.central_claim: Finance teams need fewer month-end checks and clearer approvals, not another dashboard story.
edits: Lead with what finance teams actually need rather than product adjectives.
Name close workflow details like approvals, ERP checks, and spreadsheets.
Turn the copy into an operator-facing claim instead of a generic platform description.

remaining_risks: Could name one more specific close step for an even sharper operator feel.

notes: The contrast between hype and workflow is clear.
The tone moves from hype to operator credibility.
The sequence moves cleanly from old frame to believable task fit.
